questions.prompt: What happens when a container exceeds its memory limit?

choices.text: The kernel OOM killer fires inside the container's cgroup and kills a process — usually PID 1, which exits the container with code 137. `docker inspect` shows OOMKilled. CPU limits throttle (slow down), memory limits don't — they're hard caps enforced by killing. JVM heap and Node max-old-space-size must be sized below the container limit because runtime overhead counts toward the limit.

questions.explanation: Memory limit = hard cap = OOMKilled. CPU limit = throttle. Size language heap below container limit.

questions.prompt: A JVM app runs in a container with `--memory 1g`. It gets OOMKilled at ~700MB heap. Why?

choices.text: JVM has substantial off-heap usage (metaspace, JIT code cache, thread stacks, native libs, GC structures) — typically 20-40% of total JVM memory. With `-Xmx1g` the JVM tries to use 1GB heap + overhead = ~1.4GB total, which exceeds the container limit. Fix: set heap to ~70% of container memory (`-Xmx700m` for a 1g container), or use `-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=70` and let the JVM auto-size relative to the cgroup limit. Modern JVMs read cgroup limits automatically with this flag.

questions.explanation: JVM heap + overhead must fit in container limit. Heap = ~70% of container memory; use MaxRAMPercentage on modern JVMs.
